flags.concern: Live-update cutover ops: Sprint and runbook cutover requirements conflict; the plan references the runbook but only requires one publishing-off replay, while the runbook still requires a formal Phase 1 soak/gate and 20+ replay windows.
flags.evidence: Kept the same root-cause approach, but resolved the critique by choosing a dedicated `topic_editor_runs` model, defining active-only lease semantics, adding required `db_handler.py` wrappers, adding admin/health updates, preserving the runtime injection seam, and explicitly reconciling the stale runbook gates with the sprint's no-shadow scope.

flags.concern: Topic editor run lifecycle: Plan does not choose a single run table model, alternating between `topic_editor_runs` and shared `live_update_editor_runs.metadata.editor`, which affects runbook verification, health checks, and transition/run linkage.
flags.evidence: Kept the same root-cause approach, but resolved the critique by choosing a dedicated `topic_editor_runs` model, defining active-only lease semantics, adding required `db_handler.py` wrappers, adding admin/health updates, preserving the runtime injection seam, and explicitly reconciling the stale runbook gates with the sprint's no-shadow scope.

flags.concern: Operational visibility: Admin chat and health checks remain scoped to legacy `live_update_*` tables and are not included in the cutover plan, so operators may lose accurate status after topics become primary.
flags.evidence: Kept the same root-cause approach, but resolved the critique by choosing a dedicated `topic_editor_runs` model, defining active-only lease semantics, adding required `db_handler.py` wrappers, adding admin/health updates, preserving the runtime injection seam, and explicitly reconciling the stale runbook gates with the sprint's no-shadow scope.

flags.concern: Storage integration: New topic helpers are planned only for `storage_handler.py`, but the editor is wired through `db_handler`, so missing synchronous wrapper methods would cause runtime AttributeErrors or force the new editor to bypass established DB access patterns.
flags.evidence: Kept the same root-cause approach, but resolved the critique by choosing a dedicated `topic_editor_runs` model, defining active-only lease semantics, adding required `db_handler.py` wrappers, adding admin/health updates, preserving the runtime injection seam, and explicitly reconciling the stale runbook gates with the sprint's no-shadow scope.

flags.concern: Concurrent lease schema: A generic uniqueness constraint on `(environment, guild_id, live_channel_id)` for run rows is underspecified and can block all future runs after the first completed run unless scoped to active leases.
flags.evidence: Kept the same root-cause approach, but resolved the critique by choosing a dedicated `topic_editor_runs` model, defining active-only lease semantics, adding required `db_handler.py` wrappers, adding admin/health updates, preserving the runtime injection seam, and explicitly reconciling the stale runbook gates with the sprint's no-shadow scope.

flags.concern: Did the work fully address the issue hints, user notes, and approved plan requirements?: Checked `docs/live-update-editor-sprint-plan.md` against `docs/live-update-editor-phase-2-runbook.md`. The plan correctly picks up the six locked sprint decisions, especially the run-source discriminator TODO that is now present in the runbook, but it leaves an unresolved tension: the sprint explicitly says no shadow mode/formal validation window while the runbook still requires a Phase 1 soak, 20+ replay windows, and a phase-gate signoff before cutover. Step 15 only calls for one publishing-off replay, so an executor following the runbook reference literally would hit contradictory cutover requirements.
flags.evidence: Kept the same root-cause approach, but resolved the critique by choosing a dedicated `topic_editor_runs` model, defining active-only lease semantics, adding required `db_handler.py` wrappers, adding admin/health updates, preserving the runtime injection seam, and explicitly reconciling the stale runbook gates with the sprint's no-shadow scope.

flags.concern: Are the proposed changes technically correct?: Checked the current runtime wiring in `src/features/summarising/summariser_cog.py` and storage methods in `src/common/storage_handler.py`. The plan alternates between a new `topic_editor_runs` table for runs/leases and a `live_update_editor_runs.metadata.editor` discriminator for run-source verification; those are different operational models. If the new editor writes only `topic_editor_runs`, the existing health/admin/runbook queries over `live_update_editor_runs` will not see new runs; if it writes the shared table, the migration and lease design need to say that explicitly.
flags.evidence: Kept the same root-cause approach, but resolved the critique by choosing a dedicated `topic_editor_runs` model, defining active-only lease semantics, adding required `db_handler.py` wrappers, adding admin/health updates, preserving the runtime injection seam, and explicitly reconciling the stale runbook gates with the sprint's no-shadow scope.

flags.concern: Are the proposed changes technically correct?: Checked the sprint requirement for a concurrent-run lease on `(environment, guild_id, live_channel_id)`. The plan says to add 'any lease uniqueness' on that tuple in `topic_editor_runs`, but a plain unique constraint would also block the second legitimate hourly run after the first completed. The schema needs a partial unique index over active/running rows, a separate lease table, or expiry/release columns; otherwise the lease either fails to protect concurrency or prevents future runs.
flags.evidence: Kept the same root-cause approach, but resolved the critique by choosing a dedicated `topic_editor_runs` model, defining active-only lease semantics, adding required `db_handler.py` wrappers, adding admin/health updates, preserving the runtime injection seam, and explicitly reconciling the stale runbook gates with the sprint's no-shadow scope.

The plan migrates publishing state into `topics` and `topic_transitions` but does not include admin/health tooling updates, so after cutover operator status commands could report stale legacy feed/watchlist/candidate state while the new editor is operating elsewhere.

flags.concern: Does the change touch all locations AND supporting infrastructure?: Checked how `LiveUpdateEditor` is instantiated: `SummarizerCog` receives a synchronous `db_handler` and the legacy editor calls methods like `db.get_live_update_checkpoint`, `db.search_live_update_feed_items`, and `db.insert_live_update_watchlist`. The plan adds topic helpers only to `src/common/storage_handler.py`; it does not explicitly add corresponding `src/common/db_handler.py` wrappers, so a `TopicEditor` using the same dependency shape would not be able to call the new storage methods through the object it is actually passed.

flags.concern: Topic dispatcher: Rejected-call action mapping remains ambiguous for invalid/replayed `update_topic_source_messages` and `discard_topic` calls because the locked rejected-call schema only names post/watch rejected actions.
flags.evidence: Kept the implementation direction unchanged and resolved the remaining critique by making rejected-call action mapping explicit: only post/watch create-style failures use the locked `rejected_*` actions, while invalid update/discard calls use base-action audit rows with `payload.outcome='tool_error'` and no invented rejected action names.

flags.concern: Are the proposed changes technically correct?: Checked the rejected-call schema against the v6 tool surface. The sprint locks only `rejected_post_simple`, `rejected_post_sectioned`, and `rejected_watch`, while the tool surface also includes write tools `update_topic_source_messages` and `discard_topic`; the revised plan still says every dispatcher rejection uses the locked rejected-call names without defining what happens if an update/discard call is invalid or replayed. An executor could either invent unapproved rejected actions that violate the locked schema or fail to log those rejections as required.
flags.evidence: Kept the implementation direction unchanged and resolved the remaining critique by making rejected-call action mapping explicit: only post/watch create-style failures use the locked `rejected_*` actions, while invalid update/discard calls use base-action audit rows with `payload.outcome='tool_error'` and no invented rejected action names.
